1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
5	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
6	
7	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
8	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
10	\[
11	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
12	  \qquad
13	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
14	\]
15	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
16	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
17	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
18	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
19	
20	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
21	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
22	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
23	% groups)
24	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
25	% SOURCE QUOTE:
26	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
27	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
28	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
29	% abelian groups defined by the formula
30	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
31	% \end{dfn}
32	%  [\dots]
33	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
34	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
35	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
36	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
37	% topologies  by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
39	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
40	%  \end{dfn}"
41	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
42	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
43	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
44	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
45	homomorphisms.}
46	
47	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
48	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
49	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
50	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
51	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
52	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
53	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
54	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
56	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
57	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
58	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
59	structure. This is the content of
60	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
61	
62	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
63	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
64	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
65	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
66	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
67	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
68	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
69	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
70	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
71	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
72	\begin{enumerate}
73	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
74	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
75	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
76	    isomorphism classes;
77	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
78	    for \(\otimes\);
79	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
80	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
81	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
82	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
83	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
84	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
85	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
86	\end{enumerate}
87	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
88	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
89	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
90	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
91	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
92	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
93	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
94	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
95	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
96	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
97	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
98	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
99	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
100	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
101	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
102	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
103	supplied.
104	
105	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
106	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
107	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
108	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
109	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
110	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
111	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
112	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
113	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
114	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
115	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
116	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
117	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
118	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
119	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
120	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
121	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
122	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
123	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
124	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
125	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
126	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
127	avoids. The
128	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
129	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
130	The whole \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / stalk
131	apparatus is therefore vestigial for the group law and off the critical path.
132	
133	\section{Mathlib API survey}
134	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
135	
136	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
137	(\texttt{b80f227}) were originally assembled into a single \emph{realization route}
138	(route~(e)): obtaining a full monoidal structure on the substrate by instantiating
139	Mathlib's abstract localization-of-monoidal-categories API rather than
140	hand-assembling the associator. That route is \emph{superseded}. The group law on
141	iso-classes never consumes a coherent monoidal category, and the associator is
142	obtained by direct gluing through \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, so the
143	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance on which route~(e) hinges is never required.
144	The survey below is retained as a Mathlib-API reference and to record why the route
145	is vestigial; the four Mathlib facts are all verified against the on-disk Mathlib
146	source.
147	
148	\paragraph{(i) The presheaf-of-modules monoidal category.} Mathlib's
149	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal} constructs a
150	monoidal-category instance
151	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\) on
152	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,
153	\mathtt{RingCat})\), with the sectionwise relative tensor
154	\[
155	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
156	\]
157	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct` /
158	% `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`, in
159	% `Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean` (L104, L125)
160	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean).
161	% SOURCE QUOTE:
162	% "noncomputable instance monoidalCategoryStruct :
163	%     MonoidalCategoryStruct (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) where
164	%   tensorObj := tensorObj
165	%   whiskerLeft _ _ _ g := tensorHom (𝟙 _) g
166	%   whiskerRight f _ := tensorHom f (𝟙 _)
167	%   ...
168	%   tensorUnit := unit _
169	%   associator M₁ M₂ M₃ := isoMk (fun _ ↦ α_ _ _ _) ...
170	% noncomputable instance monoidalCategory :
171	%     MonoidalCategory (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) where ..."
172	together with associator, left/right unitors and braiding making it symmetric
173	monoidal. The base presheaf of rings \(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,
174	\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat}\) is exactly the project's
175	\(\mathtt{Sheaf.val}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) carrier (equal by \(\mathtt{rfl}\)),
176	so this monoidal structure applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
177	\(\mathcal{O}_X\) --- no project work. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
178	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
179	
180	\paragraph{(ii) The localization-of-monoidal-categories API.} Mathlib's
181	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.Basic} provides the class
182	\(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) and the construction
183	\(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\). The class records exactly
184	the whiskering-stability data:
185	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `CategoryTheory.MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal` and
186	% `CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal`, in
187	% `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean` (L44, L86, L440)
188	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean).
189	% SOURCE QUOTE:
190	% "class IsMonoidal : Prop extends W.IsMultiplicative where
191	%   whiskerLeft (X : C) {Y₁ Y₂ : C} (g : Y₁ ⟶ Y₂) (hg : W g) : W (X ◁ g)
192	%   whiskerRight {X₁ X₂ : C} (f : X₁ ⟶ X₂) (hf : W f) (Y : C) : W (f ▷ Y)
193	%  ...
194	% def LocalizedMonoidal (L : C ⥤ D) (W : MorphismProperty C)
195	%     [W.IsMonoidal] [L.IsLocalization W] {unit : D} (_ : L.obj (𝟙_ C) ≅ unit) := D
196	%  ...
197	% noncomputable instance :
198	%     MonoidalCategory (LocalizedMonoidal L W ε) where ..."
199	Given \([\MonoidalCategory\,C]\), \([W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}]\), a localization
200	functor \(L : C \to D\) with \([L.\mathtt{IsLocalization}\,W]\) and an isomorphism
201	\(\varepsilon : L(\mathbf{1}_C) \cong \mathtt{unit}\), the localized category
202	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\,L\,W\,\varepsilon\) is a \(\MonoidalCategory\) with
203	\emph{all} coherence (associator, unitors, pentagon, triangle, naturalities)
204	derived. There is \emph{no} \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) anywhere in the
205	hypothesis chain.
206	
207	\paragraph{(iii) The proof template for $W$-monoidality.} Mathlib's
208	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW} proves
209	\((J.W\,(A := A)).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) \emph{stalkwise} for presheaves valued in a
210	\emph{fixed} monoidal concrete category \(A\) (i.e.\ \(C^{op} \to A\)), via the
211	conservative-family characterisation \(\mathtt{hP.W\_iff}\),
212	\(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.map\_tensor}\) and typeclass inference:
213	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `CategoryTheory.ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal_W`
214	% and the `(J.W).IsMonoidal` instance, in
215	% `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean` (L48, L57)
216	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean).
217	% SOURCE QUOTE:
218	% "lemma ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal_W
219	%     [J.HasSheafCompose (forget A)] :
220	%     (J.W (A := A)).IsMonoidal :=
221	%   .mk' _ (fun f g hf hg ↦ by
222	%     simp only [hP.W_iff (A := A)] at hf hg ⊢
223	%     intro Φ
224	%     rw [Functor.Monoidal.map_tensor]
225	%     infer_instance)"
226	This is the \emph{template} for the proof technique used in route~(e), but it does
227	\emph{not} apply directly to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\): modules over a
228	\emph{varying} ring are not of the form \(C^{op} \to A\) for a fixed monoidal
229	\(A\).
230	
231	\paragraph{The gap and route~(e), superseded.} The substrate
232	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) of this chapter is the
233	sheafification of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\), and
234	sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the localization
235	\(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\). Route~(e)
236	proposed to obtain a \emph{full} monoidal structure on
237	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
238	by applying \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) (ii) to the already-monoidal category (i),
239	which would require the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (its two
240	whiskering-stability fields \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}, with load-bearing
241	local-injectivity half \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}). This is
242	\emph{not} how the group law is realized. The group law consumes only the
243	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms, and the associator is built
244	directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through
245	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}); it never invokes
246	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), a coherent monoidal category, or the stalkwise
247	whiskering apparatus. The route~(e) instance is therefore vestigial and off the
248	critical path.
249	
250	The stalk-infrastructure analysis that route~(e) turned on is recorded here for
251	reference only, since that route is superseded. The \emph{stalk module structure}
252	is already present in Mathlib: the file
253	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk} supplies, for a topological
254	space \(X\), a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) on \(X\) and a point
255	\(x \in X\), the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure on the stalk
256	\(\mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) of a presheaf of modules \(M\),
257	together with the germ--scalar compatibility \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\); a linearity
258	packaging of the induced stalk map of a morphism was built project-side
259	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, the \emph{linearity half} of ingredient (d.1)).
260	Route~(e) additionally required a (d.1)-\emph{bridge} between the site-level
261	\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on
262	\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
263	\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor; there is
264	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. Because the group law
265	is realized through the direct-gluing associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso},
266	via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) and never through
267	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), none of this stalk apparatus is on the critical path;
268	it is retained only to document the superseded route.
269	
270	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
271	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
272	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
273	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
274	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
275	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
276	independently of any upstream timing.
277	
278	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
279	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
280	
281	\begin{definition}
282	
283	\leanok
284	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
285	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
286	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
287	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
288	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
289	  \[
290	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
291	  \]
292	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
293	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
294	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
295	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
296	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
297	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
298	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
299	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
300	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
301	  canonical compatibility
302	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
303	     \;\cong\;
304	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
305	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
306	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
307	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
308	
309	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
310	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
311	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
312	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
313	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
314	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
315	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
316	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
317	\end{definition}
318	
319	\begin{lemma}
320	
321	\leanok
322	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
323	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
324	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
325	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
326	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
327	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
328	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
329	  \[
330	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
331	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
332	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
333	  \]
334	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
335	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
336	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
337	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
338	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
339	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
340	  \[
341	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
342	    \qquad
343	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
344	  \]
345	  the associator
346	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
347	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
348	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
349	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
350	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
351	\end{lemma}
352	
353	\begin{proof}
354	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
355	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
356	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
357	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
358	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
359	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
360	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
361	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
362	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
363	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
364	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
365	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
366	\end{proof}
367	
368	\begin{remark}
369	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
370	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
371	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
372	        lem:whisker_of_W}
373	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
374	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
375	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
376	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
377	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
378	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
379	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
380	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
381	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
382	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
383	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
384	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
385	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
386	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
387	
388	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
389	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
390	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
391	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
392	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
393	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
394	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
395	  fixed-base presheaves in
396	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
397	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
398	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
399	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). But this
400	  apparatus is not on the critical path at all: the group law is realized without
401	  it.
402	
403	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
404	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
405	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
406	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
407	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
408	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
409	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
410	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
411	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
412	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
413	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
414	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
415	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
416	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
417	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
418	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
419	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
420	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
421	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
422	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
423	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
424	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
425	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
426	\end{remark}
427	
428	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
429	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
430	
431	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
432	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
433	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
434	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
435	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
436	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
437	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
438	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
439	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
440	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
441	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
442	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
443	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
444	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
445	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
446	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
447	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
448	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
449	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
450	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
451	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
452	to the off-path route~(e).
453	
454	\begin{lemma}
455	
456	\leanok
457	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
458	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
459	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
460	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
461	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
462	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
463	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
464	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
465	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
466	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
467	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
468	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
469	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
470	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
471	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
472	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
473	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
474	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
475	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
476	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
477	  \[
478	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
479	      \;\;:=\;\;
480	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
481	  \]
482	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
483	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
484	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
485	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
486	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
487	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
488	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
489	\end{lemma}
490	
491	\begin{proof}
492	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
493	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
494	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
495	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
496	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
497	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
498	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
499	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
500	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
501	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
502	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
503	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
504	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
505	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
506	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
507	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
508	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
509	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
510	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
511	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
512	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
513	
514	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
515	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
516	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
517	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
518	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
519	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
520	\end{proof}
521	
522	\begin{lemma}
523	
524	\leanok
525	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
526	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
527	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
528	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
529	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
530	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
531	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
532	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
533	  morphism
534	  \[
535	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
536	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
537	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
538	  \]
539	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
540	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
541	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
542	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
543	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
544	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
545	\end{lemma}
546	
547	\begin{proof}
548	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
549	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
550	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
551	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
552	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
553	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
554	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
555	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
556	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
557	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
558	  isomorphism}
559	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
560	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
561	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
562	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
563	  therefore extension of scalars along
564	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
565	
566	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
567	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
568	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
569	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
570	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
571	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
572	  the abstract pullback functor.
573	
574	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
575	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
576	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
577	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
578	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
579	   \;\cong\;
580	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
581	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
582	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
583	  comparison
584	  \[
585	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
586	      \;\longrightarrow\;
587	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
588	      \;\otimes\;
589	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
590	  \]
591	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
592	
593	  \emph{Step 3 (the genuine residual: H1 alone).} Completing this comparison at the
594	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise handle on the abstract functor
595	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
596	  adjoint of pushforward and admits no sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the pinned
597	  commit. The closure passes through two presheaf-level ingredients, of which
598	  exactly one now remains open.
599	
600	  The first ingredient --- a strong-monoidal upgrade of
601	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-sheaf ring \emph{isomorphism}
602	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so that base change along the isomorphism commutes with
603	  \(\otimes\) --- is \emph{closed in this chapter} as
604	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
605	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}\)): for a ring isomorphism
606	  \(e : R \simeq S\) the canonical map \(a \otimes_R b \mapsto a \otimes_S b\) is an
607	  \(R\)-linear equivalence \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R
608	  \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M
609	  \otimes_S N)\).
610	
611	  The \emph{sole remaining residual} is therefore the presheaf-level identification
612	  \[
613	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \;\cong\; \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
614	  \]
615	  via \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\). One exhibits a
616	  presheaf-level adjunction
617	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) and concludes
618	  that \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\), being a left adjoint of
619	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), is canonically isomorphic to the abstract left
620	  adjoint \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\). Here \(\beta\) is the structure map of the
621	  restriction functor, so that \((M|_f).\mathrm{val} =
622	  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,M.\mathrm{val}\) definitionally;
623	  composing the resulting comparison with
624	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv} yields the asserted isomorphism.
625	
626	  This presheaf-level adjunction is the presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
627	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\), and must be built from
628	  presheaf-level \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
629	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\). These two building blocks are \emph{Mathlib-absent}
630	  at the pinned commit: only the \emph{sheaf}-level versions exist
631	  (\texttt{Mathlib/.../SheafOfModules/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}), whereas
632	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardId}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) do already exist at the
633	  presheaf level. The depth of this sub-chain is at present uncertain.
634	
635	  This lemma is the single substrate linchpin of the relative Picard group law: the
636	  direct gluing construction of the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
637	  invokes it (twice per cover member) to descend to the canonical presheaf-level
638	  associator. Closing the H1 residual is therefore the sole remaining substrate
639	  obligation.
640	
641	  \emph{The make-or-break: only the free-cover case is on the critical path.} The
642	  statement above is proved here for \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
643	  \(M, N\), and in that generality the closure passes through the H1 residual
644	  (\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\)), whose
645	  presheaf-level building blocks are Mathlib-absent. But the associator
646	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} consumes this comparison only on a \emph{trivialising
647	  cover}, where \(M\) and \(N\) restrict to \emph{free} modules. On that free cover
648	  the comparison is the elementary, structural one: sheaf-\(\otimes\) coincides with
649	  presheaf-\(\otimes\) on the free pieces, and restriction along the open immersion is
650	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{ring isomorphism} \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\),
651	  so the isomorphism follows from
652	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv} alone, with \emph{no} appeal to the
653	  general presheaf-pushforward adjunction. The PRIMARY proof obligation of the
654	  construction is therefore the free-cover specialisation of this lemma, to be
655	  discharged \emph{without} H1; the arbitrary-module statement (with its H1 residual)
656	  is strictly stronger than the group law requires. As a fallback, should the
657	  free-cover case itself genuinely require the general adjunction H1, the by-hand
658	  route would have gained nothing over instantiating the coherent monoidal structure,
659	  and one would revert to comparing against the superseded route~(e)
660	  \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to \mathtt{Sheaf.monoidalCategory}\)
661	  (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}).
662	\end{proof}
663	
664	\begin{lemma}
665	[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with $\otimes$]
666	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
667	  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}
668	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M, N\) be
669	  \(S\)-modules. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the tensor
670	  product: the canonical map
671	  \[
672	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N)
673	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
674	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \otimes_S N\bigr),
675	    \qquad a \otimes_R b \;\longmapsto\; a \otimes_S b,
676	  \]
677	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence. Equivalently, \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\) is
678	  \emph{strong} monoidal, not merely lax, when \(e\) is a ring \emph{isomorphism}:
679	  base change along an isomorphism \(R \simeq S\) identifies the two ground rings, so
680	  the lax structure map of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) is invertible. The
681	  forward map is \(R\)-linear, but its inverse \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\)
682	  is only \emph{additive} (it is not \(S\)-linear), so the inverse is constructed as
683	  a homomorphism of additive groups and then verified to be the two-sided inverse of
684	  the forward \(R\)-linear map. This closes the strong-monoidal ingredient of
685	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, leaving the presheaf-level pushforward
686	  adjunction as the sole open residual there.
687	\end{lemma}
688	
689	\begin{lemma}
690	
691	\leanok
692	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
693	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
694	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
695	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
696	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
697	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
698	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
699	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
700	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
701	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
702	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
703	\end{lemma}
704	
705	\begin{proof}
706	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
707	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
708	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
709	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
710	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
711	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
712	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
713	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
714	\end{proof}
715	
716	\emph{Superseded route --- off path, not to be formalized.} The following lemma
717	belongs to the abandoned coherent-monoidal-category / sheafification-whiskering
718	approach to the associator. The relative Picard group law on iso-classes consumes
719	only the \emph{existence} of the associator, unitor and braiding isomorphisms
720	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}), and the associator is now built directly
721	by gluing canonical local isomorphisms (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the
722	single linchpin \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}). The whiskering /
723	\((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / stalk apparatus is never required; its Lean
724	declarations are being removed. This block is retained for the historical record
725	only and must \emph{not} be formalized.
726	
727	\begin{lemma}
728	
729	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
730	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
731	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerLeft_of_flat, PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerRight_of_flat}
732	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
733	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
734	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
735	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
736	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
737	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
738	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
739	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
740	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
741	  left-whiskered morphism
742	  \[
743	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
744	  \]
745	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
746	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
747	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
748	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
749	
750	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
751	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
752	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
753	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability data for
754	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (route~(e)) are the flatness-free variants
755	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) / \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_W}\)
756	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}), whose stalkwise proof holds for an \emph{arbitrary}
757	  whiskering object \(F\) and never invokes the sectionwise-flatness hypothesis
758	  of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over
759	  \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the site, including non-affine opens
760	  --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility or local triviality, which are
761	  affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma is therefore retained as an
762	  independent, closed result, superseded on the critical path by the
763	  \(\mathtt{\_of\_W}\) variants of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
764	\end{lemma}

766	\begin{proof}
767	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
768	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
769	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
770	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
771	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
772	
773	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
774	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
775	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
776	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
777	  \[
778	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
779	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
780	  \]
781	
782	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
783	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
784	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
785	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
786	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
787	
788	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a

730	/-! ## §1. The substrate tensor-product operation -/
731	
732	/-- **The substrate operation `⊗` on `Scheme.Modules X`.**
733	
734	For a scheme `X` and `M, N : X.Modules`, the tensor product
735	`M ⊗_X N : X.Modules` is the sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor
736	product `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` of the underlying presheaves of
737	`M` and `N` (affine-locally `(M ⊗_X N)(Spec A) = M(Spec A) ⊗_A N(Spec A)`).
738	
739	Per blueprint `def:scheme_modules_tensorobj`. iter-202 Lane TS scaffold: the
740	body is a typed `sorry`; the iter-203+ body lifts
741	`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` through the sheafification functor on
742	the small Zariski site of `X`. -/
743	noncomputable def tensorObj {X : Scheme.{u}} (M N : X.Modules) : X.Modules :=
744	  ((PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).obj
745	      (PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj (R := X.presheaf) M.val N.val) :
746	    SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf)
747	
748	/-- **Functoriality of `⊗_X`.**
749	
750	A pair of morphisms `f : M ⟶ M'` and `g : N ⟶ N'` in `X.Modules` determines a
751	morphism `f ⊗ g : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N'`, compatible with identities
752	and composition; the assignment `(M, N) ↦ tensorObj M N` thereby extends to a
753	bifunctor `X.Modules × X.Modules ⥤ X.Modules` natural in both arguments.
754	
755	Per blueprint `lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality`. iter-202 Lane TS
756	scaffold: the body is a typed `sorry`; the iter-203+ body inherits the
757	morphism action from `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` under
758	sheafification. -/
759	noncomputable def tensorObj_functoriality {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' N N' : X.Modules}
760	    (f : M ⟶ M') (g : N ⟶ N') : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N' :=
761	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).map
762	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorHom
763	      (C := _root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) f.val g.val)
764	
765	/-- **`⊗`-invertibility of an `𝒪_X`-module.** (Blueprint
766	`def:scheme_modules_isinvertible`.) `M : X.Modules` is `⊗`-invertible when it
767	admits a tensor inverse: an object `N` with `M ⊗_X N ≅ 𝒪_X`, where
768	`𝒪_X = SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf` is the designated unit. This is the
769	scheme-level analogue of Mathlib's `Module.Invertible`; the predicate carries its
770	inverse witness existentially, so the dual needed by the relative Picard group
771	law is definitional. -/
772	def IsInvertible {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) : Prop :=
773	  ∃ N : X.Modules, Nonempty (tensorObj M N ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)

962	  have hWU' : W ≤ (𝟙 X) ⁻¹ᵁ U := hWU
963	  set j : (W : Scheme) ⟶ (U : Scheme) := Scheme.Hom.resLE (𝟙 X) U W hWU' with hj
964	  have hjι : j ≫ U.ι = W.ι := by rw [hj, Scheme.Hom.resLE_comp_ι, Category.comp_id]

966	    CategoryTheory.final_of_representablyFlat _
967	  -- M.restrict W.ι ≅ (pullback W.ι).obj M
968	  refine (Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback W.ι).app M ≪≫ ?_
969	  -- ≅ (pullback (j ≫ U.ι)).obj M
970	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr hjι.symm).app M ≪≫ ?_
971	  -- ≅ (pullback j).obj ((pullback U.ι).obj M)
972	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp j U.ι).symm.app M ≪≫ ?_
973	  -- ≅ (pullback j).obj (M.restrict U.ι)

982	/-- **Substrate tensor commutes with restriction along an open immersion.**
983	
984	For an open immersion `f : Y ⟶ X` and `M N : X.Modules`,
985	`(tensorObj M N).restrict f ≅ tensorObj (M.restrict f) (N.restrict f)`.
986	
987	This is the single missing-infrastructure ingredient of `A.1.c.SubT`. It says
988	the substrate `⊗` (sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor) commutes
989	with the restriction functor `restrictFunctor f ≅ pullback f` along an open
990	immersion. Mathematically it factors as: (i) the presheaf-of-modules tensor
991	commutes with the pullback of presheaves (sectionwise this is the
992	extension-of-scalars identity
993	`B ⊗_A (P ⊗_A Q) ≅ (B ⊗_A P) ⊗_B (B ⊗_A Q)` for `A → B`, Stacks 03DM); and
994	(ii) sheafification commutes with pullback along an open immersion (the
995	restriction is exact and the small-Zariski sheafification is local). Neither is
996	in Mathlib at the `SheafOfModules` level (there is no monoidal structure on
997	`SheafOfModules`, hence no strong-monoidal pullback). A genuine construction
998	requires either the `Localization.Monoidal` transport of the monoidal structure
999	through `PresheafOfModules.sheafification.IsLocalization` together with

1100	  obtain ⟨U', hxU', hU'_aff, ⟨eN⟩⟩ := hN x

1102	    exists_isAffineOpen_mem_and_subset (X := X) (x := x) (U := U ⊓ U') ⟨hxU, hxU'⟩

1104	  have hWU' : W ≤ U' := le_trans hWsub inf_le_right

1106	  exact tensorObj_restrict_iso W.ι M N ≪≫
